title: The Year of the Yoke
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Year of the Yoke - Pastor Tito Lyro - Matthew 11_20-30.txt
outline: Introduction: The New Year as a time for reflection
Revelation to babes rather than the wise
Come to Me: the invitation of Christ
Taking on Christ's yoke — easy and light
Conclusion: Entering the new year under Christ's yoke
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Matthew 11:20-30 at the beginning of the new year, focusing on Christ's invitation to come and take His yoke. He reflects on the concept of years as a human construct before presenting Jesus' call to those who are weary — to find rest in Him whose yoke is easy and burden is light.
transcript:
## Making This the Year of the Yoke

The Chinese zodiac assigns an animal to each year. 2021, according to that system, is the year of the ox. We know that the zodiac is foolishness. But the idea of naming a year after something is not bad in itself — it helps focus the mind. I would like for us to make 2021 the year of the yoke of Christ: the year in which we truly put his yoke upon our shoulders, give him the reins of our lives, and follow him in everything.

To fully understand Jesus's gracious invitation in verses 28-30, we need to begin with the somber verses that precede it. I want us to consider three things in Matthew 11:20-30:

1. **The condemnation of indifference** (vv. 20-24) — what happens to those who refuse the yoke
2. **The adoration of sovereignty** (vv. 25-27) — Jesus's praise to the Father for the revealing of the yoke
3. **The invitation of grace** (vv. 28-30) — the call to receive the yoke

## The Condemnation of Indifference (vv. 20-24)

> *"Then He began to rebuke the cities in which most of His mighty works had been done, because they did not repent."* (Matthew 11:20)

Notice what condemned these cities. Jesus was not attacked there, was not dragged through the streets, was not viciously opposed. The cities of Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum were simply **indifferent** to him. They did not care. That was enough.

These three cities were all in Galilee, in northern Israel. Capernaum was Peter's hometown, the base of operations for Jesus's Galilean ministry. The bulk of Jesus's miracles were performed in these three cities. And they just did not care.

Jesus says:

> *"Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works which were done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes."* (Matthew 11:21)

> *"And you, Capernaum, who are exalted to heaven, will be brought down to Hades; for if the mighty works which had been done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day."* (Matthew 11:23)

A *woe* is the opposite of a blessing. It is a malediction instead of a benediction — the curse of God pronounced rather than his favor. And notice the comparison: it will be *more tolerable* on the day of judgment for Tyre, Sidon, and Sodom than for these cities.

Tyre and Sidon were the centers of Baal worship — people who sacrificed their children to Moloch. Sodom needs no introduction. And yet Jesus says: those cities are better off than people who sat under his preaching and miracles and just shrugged.

Why? Because God takes gracious opportunities into account. The more light you have been given, the greater the accountability. Rejected opportunities to believe in Christ become charges against us at the judgment. That is why Hebrews warns repeatedly: do not drift, do not harden your heart, do not neglect so great a salvation.

**Unbelief is the only sin that sends you to hell.** There is only one sin that ultimately condemns a person, and that sin is not believing in Jesus Christ. What are you doing with Jesus? Are you indifferent to him?

## The Adoration of Sovereignty (vv. 25-27)

After pronouncing these woes, Jesus suddenly turns and praises the Father:

> *"I thank You, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that You have hidden these things from the wise and prudent and have revealed them to babes. Even so, Father, for so it seemed good in Your sight."* (Matthew 11:25-26)

Jesus is not thanking the Father that he is deliberately damning people. He is speaking about the self-important — those who, by the world's standards, are wise and intelligent but who are too proud to receive the gospel as a gift. Christ has no use for the proud. He thanks his Father that their eyes are closed in their pride.

By contrast, he thanks the Father for revealing the gospel to "babes" — infants, those who are completely helpless and know it. The babes in verse 25 are the same as the burdened and weary in verse 28.

Why does God reveal to some and not to others? Verse 26: *"For so it seemed good in Your sight."* That is the answer God gives throughout Scripture. He does not owe salvation to anyone. He does not owe salvation to any particular group or individual. When we say he does, we deny the gracious nature of salvation — because if God *owes* it, it is not grace. God determined that this is good. And we, who are his creatures, receive that answer.

> *"All things have been delivered to Me by My Father, and no one knows the Son except the Father. Nor does anyone know the Father except the Son, and the one to whom the Son wills to reveal Him."* (Matthew 11:27)

This is the most offensive claim of Christianity: it is only through Christ that one can know the Father. The moment that claim is made, opposition arises. And yet Jesus says it plainly. The Father will make himself known to whom he wills — and then Jesus turns to you and says: *Come.*

## The Invitation of Grace (vv. 28-30)

> *"Come to Me, all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take My yoke upon you and learn from Me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For My yoke is easy and My burden is light."* (Matthew 11:28-30)

There are three commands in this passage: **come, take, and learn.**

**Come.** Jesus freely offers the gospel and invites you to come just as you are. J.C. Ryle writes: "He that is Christ imposes no hard conditions. He does not say anything about works to be done first, or about establishing whether we deserve his gifts. He only asks us to come to him just as we are, with all our sins, and to submit ourselves like little children to his teaching."

In John 6:35, Jesus says: *"I am the bread of life. He who comes to Me shall never hunger, and he who believes in Me shall never thirst."* To come to Jesus is to believe in him. The invitation is especially to those who are laboring and burdened under the weight of their sins. Jesus has no use for the perfect ones. He says in Matthew 9: *"Those who are well have no need of a physician... I did not come to call the righteous, but sinners, to repentance."* Are you a sinner? Are you depraved? Are you realizing there is nothing good in you? You are the prime candidate to come to Jesus.

The rest Jesus gives when we come to him is the rest of faith — knowing that all our sins have been dealt with. Horatio Spafford wrote *"It Is Well with My Soul"* after learning that his daughters had drowned in the Atlantic. As the ship crossed the waters where they went down, he found his rest in: *"My sin — oh, the bliss of this glorious thought — my sin, not in part but the whole, is nailed to the cross, and I bear it no more."*

**Take.** In the Old Testament the yoke was a symbol of subjugation — of being cursed by God, of being under an iron burden. And yet Jesus says: take my yoke upon you. He says this because he knows we will always be yoked to something. We are made to serve. The only question is: yoked to sin, or yoked to Christ?

> *"Do you not know that to whom you present yourselves slaves to obey, you are that one's slaves whom you obey, whether of sin leading to death, or of obedience leading to righteousness?"* (Romans 6:16)

When we take Christ's yoke, we become his slaves. But he is not a harsh master. He is gentle and humble. He does not drive us; he leads us as the Shepherd of Psalm 23. He does not punish us; he disciplines us to produce fruit. He will never sell us to another master. He keeps us forever.

We were made to bear his yoke. We are like the sled dog who finds his fulfillment in pulling the sled across the Yukon — that is what he was bred for. Keeping him on the couch is not kindness. Our contentment is in bearing the yoke of Christ. And his burden is light because he himself helps us carry it. Philippians 2:12-13: *"Work out your own salvation... for it is God who works in you both to will and to do for His good pleasure."*

**Learn.** J.V. McGee writes: "To be a pupil of Jesus is to have a very gentle and humble-minded teacher who is never impatient with those who are slow to learn and never intolerant with those who stumble." We come to Christ and we learn from him. The word "learn" is the root of the word "disciple." We are called to be disciples of Jesus — taught by him, shaped by him, obedient to him.

## What It Looks Like in Practice

**We are ready to work.** No farmer puts a yoke on oxen to have them sit idle. We are yoked in order to work. We rest in Christ and work for Christ — those go hand in hand. Eternity will not be sitting on clouds; it will be perfectly productive worship and service forever.

**We are yoked together with others.** Each yoke attached at least two animals. Christ binds us together so that together we can work in his service. By ourselves we cannot disciple the nations. As a church, yoked together in Christ, we can make a dent.

**The reins are in Christ's hands.** The farmer holds the reins; the animals go where he directs. When we take the yoke of Christ, we put everything in our lives under his control — our marriages, our parenting, our relationships, our use of money, our worship, our speech, our work, our entertainment. The yoke of Christ is not burdensome for any of these. It is actually simpler than we make it. What does God want from me in this relationship? He tells us clearly in his word. The yoke is easy.

The church of Jesus Christ has 2021 ahead. We do not know what is coming. But we know we can make this the year of the yoke of Christ — giving the reins of our lives to him and living in a way that shows he is King of Kings and Lord of Lords. He says his yoke is easy. Come to Jesus.
